home.subtitle: Interactive lessons that make cryptography click — type, explore, and actually understand how Bitcoin works.
home.sha256Description: Type anything and watch it hash instantly. See why even changing one character flips half the output — and try to guess a hash backwards.
home.howSha256Description: Trace "abc" through padding, message schedule expansion, and 64 rounds of compression. See exactly what transforms any input into a 256-bit fingerprint.

lesson.title: Why is SHA-256 irreversible?
lesson.subtitle: SHA-256 is the backbone of Bitcoin's security. Understanding why it's a one-way function — not just hard, but mathematically impossible to reverse — is fundamental to understanding how Bitcoin works.
lesson.hookLabel: The sentence "Hello, Bitcoin." — encoded as SHA-256
lesson.hookBody: That 64-character string encodes the phrase above. You cannot get the original sentence back from it — not in a trillion years. Here's the mathematical reason why.
lesson.s1p1: A hash function takes any input — a single character, a novel, an entire Bitcoin block — and produces a fixed-length fingerprint called a digest. SHA-256 always outputs exactly 256 bits, written as 64 hexadecimal characters.
lesson.s1prop1Body: "hello" always hashes to the same 64 characters. Always.
lesson.s1prop2Body: One byte or one gigabyte — the output is always 64 hex characters.
lesson.s1prop3Body: Computing a SHA-256 hash is cheap. Reversing one is not possible.
lesson.s1tryIt: Try it yourself below. Notice that any input — even nothing — always produces exactly 64 characters.
lesson.s2intro: "Irreversible" is not a practical limitation waiting for faster computers. It's baked into the mathematical structure. Three separate arguments explain why.
lesson.step1p1: SHA-256 maps an infinite set of possible inputs onto a finite set of 2²⁵⁶ outputs. By the pigeonhole principle, infinitely many inputs share every possible output hash.
lesson.step1p2: When you see a hash, you can't know which input produced it. You might find a preimage by brute force — but never the original. That information is gone.
lesson.step1callout: 2²⁵⁶ ≈ 10⁷⁷ — larger than the estimated number of atoms in the observable universe. Even checking one hash per atom per second, exhaustive search is physically impossible.
lesson.step2Title: The avalanche effect (no gradient to follow)
lesson.step2p1: Change a single character — flip one bit — and roughly half of all output bits change, apparently at random.
lesson.step2p2: There's no "getting closer." Every wrong guess looks equally wrong. Unlike a numeric equation you can solve iteratively, you have zero signal to guide a search.
lesson.step3p1: SHA-256 uses bitwise <c>XOR</c>, <c>AND</c>, <c>OR</c>, <c>bit rotation</c>, and <c>modular addition</c> — run through 64 rounds, each feeding into the next. Unlike RSA (which has a mathematical inverse) or a Caesar cipher (just reverse the shift), there is no inverse operation for SHA-256.
lesson.step3p2: Each <c>modular addition</c> discards carry information. Each <c>XOR</c> can be satisfied by two different input pairs. After 64 rounds, reconstructing the input is equivalent to solving a massively underdetermined system of equations.
lesson.s3body: Try a long paragraph, a single space, your name. No matter the size, the output is always exactly 64 hex characters — and nothing about it tells you how to find the input.
lesson.s4Body: Below is the SHA-256 hash of a short phrase. Figure out what the input was. No tricks — the only method that works is guessing.
lesson.s4callout: Even matching 63 out of 64 characters is a complete miss. That's what "preimage resistance" means — hash functions are all-or-nothing.
lesson.s5intro: SHA-256's irreversibility is not a footnote — it's the foundation Bitcoin is built on.
lesson.s5miningBody: Miners must find a block header whose hash starts with a certain number of zeros. Because hashing is irreversible, there's no shortcut — they try billions of variations per second. That brute-force effort is the "work" in Proof of Work.
lesson.s5addressBody: Your private key is hashed to produce your public address. Even though your address is visible on the blockchain, the hash ensures no one can work backwards to your private key — and your funds.
lesson.s5callout: Every Bitcoin block contains the SHA-256 hash of the previous block. This chain of hashes makes rewriting history computationally impossible — you'd have to redo the proof-of-work for every block that follows.

howSha256.title: How does SHA-256 work?
howSha256.subtitle: SHA-256 turns any input into a 256-bit fingerprint using three deterministic phases. Tracing those steps reveals exactly why the output looks random — and why that's by design.
howSha256.hookBody: Three characters. Three phases. The 64 hex characters above are the inevitable result of running "abc" through a specific sequence of bit operations. Here's every step.
howSha256.s1intro: Every SHA-256 computation follows the same recipe. The input can be one byte or one gigabyte — the same three phases always run in the same order.
howSha256.s1phase1Body: Extend the message to an exact multiple of 512 bits using a structured pattern.
howSha256.s1phase2Body: Expand the padded block from 16 words into a 64-word schedule of mixed values.
howSha256.s1phase3Body: Run 8 working variables through 64 rounds of bit operations, then output the final 256-bit hash.
howSha256.s1tryIt: Try typing anything below. The same three phases run every time — instantly, deterministically.
howSha256.s2intro: SHA-256 processes data in 512-bit (64-byte) blocks. Before hashing begins, the message is extended to fit this block structure using a precise three-part scheme.
howSha256.step1p1: The message is encoded as bytes. ASCII text is straightforward: each character is one byte. "abc" becomes three bytes — 0x61, 0x62, 0x63.
howSha256.step1p2: The byte values are fixed by the UTF-8 standard. This step is just representation — the actual padding starts next.
howSha256.step2p1: Immediately after the last message byte, append the byte 0x80 (binary: 10000000). This marks exactly where the message ends.
howSha256.step2p2: Then append zero bytes until only 8 bytes remain in the final block. The separator guarantees that "abc" and "abc\x00" pad differently — they can't produce the same block.
howSha256.step3p1: The final 8 bytes store the original message length in bits as a 64-bit big-endian integer. "abc" is 3 bytes = 24 bits, so those last 8 bytes encode 0x0000000000000018.
howSha256.step3callout: If the message is too long to fit in one 512-bit block with the padding appended, SHA-256 uses multiple blocks — each compressed in sequence.
howSha256.s3intro: The 512-bit padded block is split into 16 × 32-bit words. SHA-256 then extends these into a 64-word schedule — one word per compression round — by mixing earlier words together.
howSha256.step4p1: Divide the 512-bit block into sixteen 32-bit chunks: W[0] through W[15]. For "abc" padded: W[0] = 0x61626380 (the first four bytes), ..., W[15] = 0x00000018 (the 24-bit message length).
howSha256.step5p1: For each position i from 16 to 63, compute a new word by mixing four earlier words. The functions σ0 and σ1 are each a XOR of three different bit rotations and shifts:
howSha256.step5callout: By W[63], every word has been mixed with every other word. A single-bit change in the original message cascades through the entire 64-word schedule.
howSha256.s4intro: The compression function uses 8 working variables — a through h — and runs 64 rounds, consuming one schedule word and one round constant per round.
howSha256.step6p1: The 8 variables are set to the first 32 bits of the fractional parts of the square roots of the first 8 primes. These "nothing-up-my-sleeve" numbers prove no backdoor was inserted — anyone can verify them.
howSha256.step7p1: Each round uses one message schedule word W[i] and one of 64 round constants K[i] (derived from the cube roots of primes). Six operations mix the 8 variables each round — the same operations from the irreversibility lesson.
howSha256.step8p1: After all 64 rounds, add each working variable back to its initial H value (mod 2³²). The 8 resulting 32-bit words concatenated form the 256-bit hash.
howSha256.step8callout: Processing multiple 512-bit blocks? The output hash from one block replaces H[0]–H[7] as the initial state for the next. Every block influences the final output.
howSha256.s5intro: Bitcoin uses SHA-256 in nearly every component — typically applied twice in sequence (called SHA256d or double-SHA-256).
howSha256.s5miningBody: Miners search for a block header whose SHA256d hash starts with enough zero bits. There's no shortcut — the only method is changing the nonce and rehashing billions of times per second.
howSha256.s5txBody: Every transaction is identified by the SHA256d of its raw bytes. This TxID is what wallets and block explorers show when referencing specific transactions.
howSha256.s5merkleBody: All transactions in a block are hashed in pairs repeatedly until one root hash remains. This Merkle root lets anyone prove a transaction is in a block without downloading the whole block.
howSha256.s5addressBody: Your private key is hashed with SHA-256 (then RIPEMD-160) to produce your Bitcoin address. The hash is irreversible — your address reveals nothing about your private key.
howSha256.s5callout: SHA-256 was designed by the NSA and standardized by NIST in 2001. Its security relies entirely on the mathematical properties you just traced — not on keeping the algorithm secret.

padding.multiBlockNote: This spans {blocks} blocks — SHA-256 compresses each in sequence.

challenge.title: Reverse Challenge
challenge.subtitle: can you find the input?
challenge.targetLabel: 🎯 Target hash — find the input that produced this
challenge.placeholder: What do you think the input was?
challenge.correctNote: You found it by guessing — not by reversing the hash. There's no algorithm that works backwards. The only method is trial and error.
challenge.wrongSubtitle: Hash functions are all-or-nothing — no partial credit.
challenge.matchCountSuffix: of 64 characters matched — even 63/64 is a complete miss.

operations.title: Operations Explorer
operations.subtitle: 8-bit demo · SHA-256 uses 32-bit
operations.rotrDesc: SHA-256 computes S0 = rotr(a,2) ^ rotr(a,13) ^ rotr(a,22). This 8-bit demo uses rotations of 1, 3, and 5 to keep it visual. Click bits to flip them.
operations.rotrWhy: A single rotation is reversible — just rotate back. But SHA-256 XORs three different rotations together. Once merged with XOR, you can't tell which bits came from which rotation. Recovering a from S0 requires solving three entangled equations simultaneously — there is no algebraic shortcut.
operations.chDesc: For each bit position: if e = 1, copy the bit from f. If e = 0, copy from g. The small label under each output bit shows which source was used.
operations.chWhy: The selector e is not present in the Ch output. Given the result and knowing f and g, you cannot recover e — you need e to know which source each bit came from, but e is exactly what you're trying to find. Swapping e and exchanging f/g produces the same Ch output from entirely different inputs.
operations.addDesc: SHA-256 uses 32-bit arithmetic (mod 2³²). This demo uses 8-bit (mod 256) so you can see the carry bit disappear. Drag the sliders until the sum overflows.
operations.addOverflow: That's ≥ 256, so the carry bit (left of divider) is discarded and the result wraps to {result}.
operations.addWhy: When the sum overflows, the carry bit is permanently gone. Given only the result {result}, you cannot reconstruct a or b — ({result}, 0) and (0, {result}) both hash to the same value, and so do every overflow pair like ({a}, {b}). SHA-256 chains 5 modular additions per round across 64 rounds, making the information loss total.
operations.majDesc: Each output bit is 1 when at least 2 of the 3 inputs are 1 — a majority vote. The small number under each result bit shows how many inputs voted 1. Click bits to experiment.
operations.majWhy: Like Ch, if you know the result is 1, you can't be sure if the inputs were (1,1,0), (1,0,1), (0,1,1), or (1,1,1). Information is lost in the 'voting' process.
operations.andExplain: AND (&): 1 only if both bits are 1.
operations.xorExplain: XOR (^): 1 if the bits are different.

roundSteps.title: Inside one SHA-256 Round
roundSteps.step1Desc: Rotate <c>e</c> by 6, 11, and 25 bits, then <c>XOR</c> them. This ensures every bit affects many others.
roundSteps.step2Desc: Use bits of <c>e</c> to choose between bits of <c>f</c> and <c>g</c>.
roundSteps.step3Desc: Sum <c>h</c>, <c>S1</c>, <c>Ch</c>, and the round's specific <c>word</c> and <c>constant</c>.
roundSteps.step4Desc: Rotate <c>a</c> by 2, 13, and 22 bits, then <c>XOR</c> them.
roundSteps.step5Desc: For each bit position, take the value that appears most often in <c>a</c>, <c>b</c>, and <c>c</c>.
roundSteps.step6Desc: Shift all values and compute new <c>a</c> and <c>e</c> for the next round.

footer.support: Enjoying the app? A few sats via Lightning are like buying me a coffee — thank you.
